name: SL Exam Phase 1 Python Core Source
description: Topic map and exam-like single-choice question bank generated from lectures 01-03. Python semantics only.

topics.title: Introduction into Python
keypoints: Python files use .py extension; run with py app.py or a selected version such as py --3.14 app.py.
Python uses indentation instead of braces; 4 spaces are recommended.
pycodestyle checks PEP8 compliance; install it with pip install pycodestyle.
PEP means Python Enhancement Proposal; PEP20 contains principles such as readability counts.
Python is dynamically typed: variable names can refer to objects of different types during execution.
Python is strongly typed: '2' + 2 raises TypeError; explicit conversion is needed.
print() is a function in Python 3; input() reads a line from standard input and returns a string.
Basic types include int, bool, float, complex; strings are sequence types.
if/else controls conditional execution; def defines a function; return gives back a result; pass creates an empty body.
for iterates over values; range produces a sequence of integers; strings can be iterated character by character.
Everything in Python is an object, with identity, type, and value.
exam_traps: Do not answer using Java/C#/JavaScript rules; use Python semantics only.
Python 3 forbids Python 2 print statement syntax: print 'Hello'.
Dynamic typing does not mean weak typing.
input() returns text, even if the user types digits.

topics.title: My first simple program
keypoints: Standard input and output are streams; applications can be sources, sinks, or filters.
< redirects input, > redirects output replacing the file, >> appends output, and | pipes one program into another.
print() writes to standard output; input() reads one line from standard input.
sys.stdin and fileinput.input() can read streams line by line.
Strings are str objects; single quotes, double quotes, and triple quotes create string literals.
Adjacent string literals are concatenated: 'abc' 'def' becomes 'abcdef'.
Strings are immutable and Python has no separate character type; a character is a string of length 1.
str(x) converts non-string objects to strings.
f-strings and str.format() format text.
strip(), join(), and split() are common string methods.
Strings are iterable; iterating over 'abc' produces 'a', 'b', 'c'.
Lists are ordered, mutable, iterable compound data types.
append() adds one element; remove() removes by value; + creates a new list.
List slicing uses start inclusive, stop exclusive; negative indexes count from the end.
A debugger runs code step by step and lets you inspect values; breakpoints stop execution.
Logging is better than print debugging for persistent diagnostic messages.
Logging levels: DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL; WARNING is the default threshold.
exam_traps: A list containing one string ['abcd'] iterates once; the string 'abcd' iterates four times.
> replaces an output file, while >> appends.
data + [x] creates a new list; append modifies the existing list.
WARNING level does not show DEBUG messages.

topics.title: Lists and tuples
keypoints: uv can initialize a project, add dependencies, sync dependencies, and run a Python file.
uv keeps project configuration in pyproject.toml and installed dependency state in uv.lock.
Positional arguments without default values must be provided.
Keyword/default arguments can be omitted and can be called by name in any order.
After a keyword argument in a call, following arguments must also be keyword arguments.
Python type hints are annotations; they can be ignored at runtime.
mypy performs static code verification; missing types are ignored by default.
Lists are mutable sequences; append adds one object, insert inserts before index, extend appends elements from an iterable.
del removes by index or slice; pop removes and returns; remove deletes the first matching value.
list.sort() sorts in place and returns None; sorted() returns a new list and leaves the original intact.
range is an immutable sequence type used mostly in for loops.
range(start, stop, step) excludes stop; range(15,0,-5) gives 15, 10, 5.
List comprehensions build lists compactly, e.g. [x*x for x in range(10)].
Tuples are immutable sequences.
A single-element tuple requires a comma, e.g. ('Brian',) or 'Brian',.
tuple([1,2,3]) gives (1,2,3); tuple('123') gives ('1','2','3'); tuple(1,2,3) is invalid.
Tuples are compared element by element; lambda can be used as a key for selected-element sorting.
A tuple has no sort() method; sorted(tuple_obj) returns a new list.
exam_traps: append([4,5,6]) adds one list object; extend([4,5,6]) adds three elements.
sorted() does not modify the original list or tuple.
('Brian') is a string; ('Brian',) is a tuple.
Type hints do not enforce runtime types by themselves.

questions.question: Which extension is normally used for Python source files?
questions.explanation: Python source files conventionally use the .py extension.

questions.question: Which command is a valid way to run a Python file named app.py on Windows using the Python launcher?
questions.explanation: The slides show py app.py as a normal way to run a Python app.

questions.question: What does Python use to define blocks of code?
questions.explanation: Python uses indentation instead of brackets/braces for blocks.

questions.question: How many spaces did the lecture recommend for indentation?
questions.explanation: The lecture encouraged using 4 spaces.

questions.question: Which tool checks Python code for compliance with PEP8 style?
questions.explanation: pycodestyle checks PEP8 compliance; it was formerly known as pep8.

questions.question: What does PEP stand for in Python?
questions.explanation: PEP means Python Enhancement Proposal.

questions.question: Python is dynamically typed. What does this mean?

choices.A: A variable name can later refer to an object of a different type
choices.B: Python automatically converts every type in every expression
choices.D: Variables must declare their type before use

questions.answer: A variable name can later refer to an object of a different type
questions.explanation: Dynamic typing means the name can refer to objects of different types during execution.

questions.question: What happens in Python 3 when evaluating 2 + '2'?
questions.explanation: Python is strongly typed; int and str are not implicitly added together.

questions.question: What type does input() return in Python?

choices.C: A type automatically detected from user input

questions.explanation: input() reads a line of text; numeric conversion must be explicit.

questions.question: Which statement about print in Python 3 is correct?

choices.B: print is a keyword statement like in Python 2

questions.explanation: In Python 3, print() is a function.

questions.question: Which keyword defines a function in Python?
questions.explanation: Function definitions start with def.

questions.question: Which keyword returns a result from a Python function?
questions.explanation: return returns a value from a function.

questions.question: Which keyword can be used as an empty function body?
questions.explanation: pass is a no-operation statement used where a body is required.

questions.question: What does this loop print first: for c in 'abcd': print(c)?
questions.explanation: Iterating over a string gives one character at a time.

questions.question: According to Python semantics, every object has:
questions.explanation: The lecture states that every object has identity, type, and value.

questions.question: In the Unix stream model, an application that receives data from STDIN, processes it, and sends the result to STDOUT is called a:
questions.explanation: A filter reads from standard input, processes, and writes to standard output.

questions.question: What does > do in a command such as py app.py > out.txt?

choices.A: Redirects output and replaces the file content if it exists

questions.answer: Redirects output and replaces the file content if it exists
questions.explanation: > redirects output to a file and replaces existing content.

questions.question: Which operator appends output to an existing file?
questions.explanation: >> appends to the original content of the file.

questions.question: Which operator pipes data from one program to another?
questions.explanation: The pipe operator connects one program's output to another program's input.

questions.question: Which Python function sends data to standard output?
questions.explanation: print() writes to standard output.

questions.question: Which Python function reads one line from standard input?
questions.explanation: input() reads one line from standard input.

questions.question: Which class handles strings in Python?
questions.explanation: Strings in Python are handled by the str class.

questions.question: What is the result of: a = 'abc' 'def'?
questions.explanation: Sibling string literals are concatenated automatically.

questions.question: Which statement about Python strings is true?

choices.B: Strings are mutable lists of characters
choices.C: Python has a separate char type

questions.explanation: Strings are immutable; there is no separate character type.

questions.question: Which expression is an f-string?
questions.explanation: An f-string begins with f before the opening quote.

questions.question: What is the result of ' too long '.strip()?
questions.explanation: strip() removes leading and trailing whitespace.

questions.question: What is the result of 'apples and pears'.split()?
questions.explanation: split() without arguments splits on whitespace.

questions.question: What is printed first by: for character in 'abc': print(character)?
questions.explanation: A string is iterable and yields one-character strings.

questions.question: Which statement about Python lists is correct?
questions.explanation: Lists are ordered mutable compound data types.

questions.question: After data = [1, 2, 3]; data.append(4), what is data?
questions.explanation: append() modifies the list by adding the object at the end.

questions.question: What does data + [6, 7, 8] do?
questions.explanation: The + operator creates a new concatenated list.

questions.question: For data = [3, 4, 45, 26, 1], what is data[-1]?
questions.explanation: Index -1 refers to the last element.

questions.question: For data = [3, 4, 45, 26, 1], what is data[1:3]?
questions.explanation: Slicing includes start index 1 and excludes stop index 3.

questions.question: What is a debugger used for?

choices.A: Running an application step by step and inspecting values

questions.answer: Running an application step by step and inspecting values
questions.explanation: A debugger allows step-by-step execution and value inspection.

questions.question: What is a breakpoint?

choices.A: A place where code execution stops

questions.answer: A place where code execution stops
questions.explanation: Breakpoints mark where the debugger should stop execution.

questions.question: Which logging level is the default threshold mentioned in the lecture?
questions.explanation: WARNING is the default logging level.

questions.question: If the logging level is WARNING, which messages are normally shown?
questions.explanation: A logging threshold shows messages at that level and more severe ones.

questions.question: Which tool does the lecture use to set up Python projects with one tool?
questions.explanation: The lecture presents uv as the single tool for project setup.

questions.question: Which file keeps Python project configuration when using uv?
questions.explanation: uv keeps configuration in pyproject.toml.

questions.question: Which uv-related file should be committed to version control according to the lecture?
questions.explanation: The lecture states that uv.lock should be committed.

questions.question: Given def foo(a, b): return a+b, what happens when calling foo(2)?
questions.explanation: Both positional arguments are required because no default value is defined.

questions.question: Given def foo(a=2, b=2): return a+b, which call is valid?
questions.explanation: Keyword arguments can be reordered.

questions.question: Which rule about Python function calls is correct?

choices.A: After a keyword argument, subsequent arguments must also be keyword arguments
choices.B: Positional arguments can always appear after keyword arguments
choices.C: Keyword arguments cannot have default values

questions.answer: After a keyword argument, subsequent arguments must also be keyword arguments
questions.explanation: The lecture states that all following arguments after a keyword argument must also be keyword arguments.

questions.question: What is true about Python type hints?

choices.A: They are annotations and can be ignored at runtime
choices.B: They always enforce type checks at runtime
choices.C: They are required in every Python function

questions.answer: They are annotations and can be ignored at runtime
questions.explanation: Type hints are annotations; runtime Python does not enforce them automatically.

questions.question: Which module is used for static code verification in the lecture?
questions.explanation: mypy is used for static code verification.

questions.question: Lists in Python are:
questions.explanation: Lists are mutable sequences indexed by numbers.

questions.question: If a = [1,2,3] and a.append([4,5,6]) is executed, what is len(a)?
questions.explanation: append adds the whole list [4,5,6] as one element.

questions.question: If a = [1,2,3] and a.extend([4,5,6]) is executed, what is len(a)?
questions.explanation: extend adds each element from the iterable.

questions.question: Which list method removes and returns the last element when no index is provided?
questions.explanation: s.pop() removes and returns the last element.

questions.question: Which list operation removes the first matching value x?
questions.explanation: remove(x) deletes the first occurrence of x from the list.

questions.question: What does list.sort() return?
questions.explanation: sort() sorts the list in place and returns None.

questions.question: What does sorted(a) do?

choices.A: Returns a new sorted list and leaves a intact
choices.B: Sorts a in place and returns None

questions.answer: Returns a new sorted list and leaves a intact
questions.explanation: sorted() returns a new sorted list; the original argument remains intact.

questions.question: What values are produced by range(0, 15, 5)?
questions.explanation: The stop value 15 is excluded.

questions.question: What values are produced by range(15, 0, -5)?
questions.explanation: With negative step, values decrease and stop before 0.

questions.question: What is created by [x*x for x in range(3)]?
questions.explanation: range(3) gives 0, 1, 2, and their squares are 0, 1, 4.

questions.question: Tuples in Python are:
questions.explanation: Tuples are immutable sequences.

questions.question: Which expression creates a single-element tuple?
questions.explanation: The comma creates the single-element tuple; ('Brian') is just a string in parentheses.

questions.question: What is len(tuple('123'))?
What happens with tuple(1, 2, 3)?
questions.explanation: tuple() expects at most one iterable argument, not three separate arguments.

questions.question: Given comps = [('Lenovo', 2499), ('HP', 2099)], what does sorted(comps, key=lambda comp: comp[1]) sort by?

choices.A: The second element of each tuple
choices.B: The first element of each tuple
choices.C: The length of the list only
choices.D: It cannot sort lists of tuples

questions.answer: The second element of each tuple
questions.explanation: The lambda returns comp[1], so the key is the price/second element.

questions.question: What is the result type of sorted((3, 1, 2))?
questions.explanation: sorted() always returns a new list, even when the input is a tuple.

questions.question: What is the result of calling (3, 1, 2).sort()?

choices.A: AttributeError because tuple has no sort method

questions.answer: AttributeError because tuple has no sort method
questions.explanation: Tuples are immutable and do not provide sort(). Use sorted(tuple_obj) for a new list.
